title: Helosciadium × longipedunculatum
intro: Helosciadium × longipedunculatum, commonly known as Long-stalked Celery-leaved Ferula, is a plant species within the carrot family, Apiaceae. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities, contributing to its potential health benefits in treating various conditions such as arthritis, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Helosciadium × longipedunculatum include volatile oils, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make various herbal preparations, including essential oils, teas, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory and digestive issues, as well as to relieve pain and inflammation.
taxonomy: Helosciadium × longipedunculatum is a hybrid of two species, Helosciadium nodiflorum and Heracleum sphondylium. It belongs to the family Apiaceae, commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. Further classification places it in the tribe Oenantheae. The genus Helosciadium is a group of flowering plants, and Helosciadium × longipedunculatum is the only hybrid species within this genus. This hybrid is native to Europe, where it can be found in a variety of wetland habitats.

side_effects: Helosciadium × longipedunculatum, also known as Fool's Parsley, is a plant that has been mistaken for the edible herb parsley due to its similarity in appearance. However, it contains a toxic compound called apiol, which can cause serious health problems if ingested. Ingestion of Fool's Parsley can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and tremors, and in severe cases, it can cause seizures and even death. Prolonged exposure to the toxic compounds in Fool's Parsley can also lead to liver damage and other systemic health issues. It is essential to avoid consuming Fool's Parsley to prevent these potentially life-threatening side effects.
